parts.content:
Окей, давай разберём этот memory leak без скороговорки. Симптомы, которые ты описал — постепенный, ровный рост heap без явных пиков, OOM где-то на двенадцатом-двадцать четвёртом часе аптайма — это классическая картина утечки через долгоживущие референсы, а не одноразового всплеска. Дальше я пройдусь по шагам: подтверждение, что это действительно leak, снятие heap dump, поиск источника, фикс и финальная проверка под нагрузкой.

# 1. Подтверди, что это именно leak

Прежде чем нырять в исходники, убедись, что рост памяти не объясняется легитимными причинами. Сначала проверь параметры GC: какой коллектор, какие размеры регионов, какой target pause time. Если ты сидишь на G1 с большим heap, рост до восьмидесяти-девяноста процентов — часто нормальное поведение перед major collection, и реальной утечки может не быть. Сними hist-stats через jcmd VM.native_memory или jstat несколько раз с интервалом в час и сравни счётчики long-lived объектов в old generation. Если они растут монотонно при стабильном RPS — это leak. Если плавают вверх-вниз — скорее всего просто кэширование, и беспокоиться не о чём.

Вторая полезная проверка — посмотреть в GC log на размер heap сразу после major collection. Если он стабильно растёт от одного major к другому, значит, есть объекты, которые не освобождаются. Если он возвращается примерно к одному уровню — у тебя просто плотный workload, а не утечка.

# 2. Heap dump в момент проблемы

Когда подтвердил, что это утечка, сделай heap dump на пике — желательно прямо перед OOM, через jcmd с командой GC.heap_dump. Можно поставить -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError, чтобы дамп снимался автоматически в момент крашa. Открой полученный hprof в Eclipse MAT — он по-прежнему золотой стандарт для такого анализа — и запусти Leak Suspects Report. MAT покажет dominator tree: какие объекты держат основной объём, и через какие GC roots они достижимы. В девяти случаях из десяти ответ будет: некий ConcurrentHashMap или ArrayList, привязанный к static-полю или к долгоживущему синглтону, копит элементы без чистки.

# 3. Найди источник

Когда видишь подозрительную коллекцию, посмотри на её ключи и значения. Если это запросы с user-id в качестве ключа, проверь, есть ли где-то eviction policy. Если ключи — ad-hoc объекты с правильным equals/hashCode, но без TTL — это твой клиент. Скорее всего это либо самописный кэш без размерного лимита, либо временный Map, который кто-то добавил для дебага и забыл убрать, либо listener registry, в который класс подписывается, но никогда не отписывается. Отдельный частый виновник — ThreadLocal, который не вычищается в конце задачи: при использовании thread pool такие значения живут столько же, сколько живёт сам пул.

# 4. Фикс

Универсальный фикс — переключить кэш на что-то с bounded capacity и TTL. Caffeine — выбор по умолчанию для in-process кэшей в JVM-мире: даёт W-TinyLFU, размерные ограничения и expireAfterWrite/expireAfterAccess из коробки. Если ты держишь registry listener-ов, используй WeakHashMap или явно вызывай unsubscribe в lifecycle hooks. Если это просто забытая коллекция — удали её, и тестов это не сломает (а если сломает — у тебя были скрытые зависимости, которые тоже надо чинить).

# 5. Проверь

После фикса не верь себе на слово. Прогон под load в течение нескольких часов с мониторингом heap usage — обязательная процедура. Если за восемь часов под нагрузкой память стабильна — фикс работает. Если опять растёт — у тебя была не одна утечка, а несколько, и надо повторить процесс с новым heap dump. Хорошая привычка — оставить в проде continuous profiler (async-profiler или Java Flight Recorder), чтобы следующая утечка диагностировалась за минуты, а не дни.

Let me give you a proper architectural walkthrough of an async processing pipeline, because there are a lot of moving parts and most of them only become visible when you reach production scale. I'll cover the conceptual model, the trade-offs between the common topologies, the failure modes you should design for from day one, the observability story, and a concrete migration path if you're moving from a synchronous request-response API.

# 1. Conceptual model

At its core, an async pipeline separates *what* needs to happen from *when* it happens and on *which* compute resources. The synchronous version is a straight line: the caller invokes a function, the function executes on the caller's thread, and the result returns when the function completes. This is fine until either the work becomes long enough that holding a thread is expensive, or the upstream becomes unreliable enough that you need retries, timeouts, and fallbacks that don't block the caller indefinitely.

The async version introduces a queue between the caller and the worker. The caller publishes a message and receives an acknowledgement immediately. A pool of workers consumes the queue and processes the messages asynchronously. The result is reported back through a separate channel — another queue, a callback URL, a polled status endpoint, or a websocket push. The caller and worker can now scale, restart, and fail independently of each other, which is the whole point of the exercise.

# 2. Topologies

There are three common topologies, and the choice between them dominates almost every other decision.

The first is the work queue: one queue, many competing consumers, each message processed once. This is the simplest and most flexible model. It's what RabbitMQ direct exchanges and SQS standard queues implement. Pros: trivial to reason about, easy to scale, easy to retry. Cons: no ordering across messages, and you need to make every message idempotent because at-least-once delivery means the same message can be processed twice.

The second is the partitioned log: messages are grouped by key, and all messages with the same key go to the same consumer in order. Kafka and Kinesis implement this. Pros: per-key ordering, replayability, multiple independent consumer groups can read the same data. Cons: rebalancing is painful, partition count is hard to change after the fact, and a slow consumer for one key can block all keys in the same partition.

The third is the fanout: one event, many independent subscribers, each with their own queue. SNS-with-SQS, EventBridge, and Kafka with multiple consumer groups all do this. Pros: loose coupling between producers and consumers, easy to add new subscribers without changing the producer. Cons: harder to reason about end-to-end behaviour because the effect of a single event is now distributed across many services.

For most internal systems you'll start with a work queue, switch to a partitioned log when you need ordering, and add fanout when you need to decouple multiple downstream services from one source of truth.

# 3. Failure modes

Three failure modes dominate the operational picture. Designing for them up front is much cheaper than retrofitting later.

The slow consumer: queue depth grows unbounded, latency drifts up, and eventually you run out of memory or start dropping messages. Mitigation: back-pressure that the producer can observe, dead-letter queues for poison messages, and alerts on queue depth and consumer lag rather than just on consumer health.

The duplicate delivery: at-least-once is the default for almost every modern broker, which means every consumer must be idempotent. The cheapest implementation is an idempotency key on the message and a small store of recently-processed keys; the more expensive but more correct one is a transactional outbox that ties the side effect to the consumer offset commit.

The permanent failure: a message that will never succeed no matter how many times you retry. Without a dead-letter queue, the message blocks the partition forever (in the log model) or gets retried indefinitely (in the queue model). Always have a DLQ, always alert on DLQ depth, and always have a runbook for what to do when DLQ alerts fire.

# 4. Observability

In the synchronous version, a stack trace tells you almost everything. In the async version, the stack trace tells you that the message was published — and nothing about what happened after. You need three things to recover the visibility you lost: correlation IDs propagated through every message, distributed tracing that spans the broker as a real participant (most modern tracing libraries support this), and per-stage metrics that let you see queue depth, processing rate, error rate, and latency as a histogram, not just an average.

The other observability cost is that your alerts need to change. In the synchronous version you alert on error rate and latency. In the async version you also alert on queue depth, consumer lag, DLQ depth, and the age of the oldest unprocessed message. The last one is the most important — it's the only metric that directly tells you whether the system is keeping up.

# 5. Migration path

If you're moving from synchronous to async, don't try to flip the entire system at once. Start by introducing the queue but keeping the call synchronous from the caller's perspective: the caller publishes a message and then polls the result endpoint until it returns. This lets you put a queue in front of the slow step without changing the caller's mental model. Once that's stable, you can teach the caller to fire and forget, and to receive results through a webhook or websocket.

The second migration step is to make the consumer truly independent: separate process, separate deployment, separate scaling policy. This is where most of the operational value lives — the consumer can now restart, scale, and roll back without touching the producer.

The third step is to split the consumer into multiple stages connected by queues, each stage doing one thing well. This is the topology that scales to large teams, because each stage can be owned by a different group with a clear contract at the queue boundary.

None of this is hard in isolation. The difficulty is that all of it has to work together, and the failure modes only show up under load. Build the observability first, test the failure modes deliberately, and you'll have a system that genuinely runs itself.

Alright, let's go deep on migrating a mature monolith to an event-driven architecture, because the way this is usually described in conference talks bears almost no resemblance to what actually happens in practice. I'll walk through the honest preconditions, the staging of work, the patterns that survive contact with production, the patterns that look elegant on a whiteboard and explode at scale, the team-shape implications, and the failure scenarios you should rehearse before you commit.

# 1. The honest preconditions

Before you start, ask three questions, and if any answer is no, you are not ready.

First: do you have a working observability stack — structured logs, metrics, distributed tracing — that already spans the existing monolith? If not, you cannot migrate. The moment you introduce the broker as an intermediate hop, the synchronous stack trace stops being your debugging tool, and you need correlation IDs propagated end to end before you can survive the first production incident.

Second: do you have a deployment pipeline that can ship multiple services independently, with feature flags, canaries, and a one-button rollback? If not, you will turn one deployable into ten, each one harder to ship than the original, and your release cadence will collapse.

Third: does the team understand the consistency model they are about to inherit? Synchronous systems give you transactional boundaries that are easy to reason about. Event-driven systems give you eventual consistency, idempotency requirements, and a distributed-state mental model. If the team is not prepared for this conceptual shift, the architecture will be technically correct and operationally cursed.

If all three are yes, you can proceed. If any is no, fix that first. The migration will go ten times faster from a healthy starting point.

# 2. Staging the work

The most common mistake is to start by carving up the monolith into microservices on day one. Don't. The right first move is to introduce events while keeping the deployable monolithic. Pick one domain — orders, inventory, billing — and start publishing domain events as a side effect of the existing transactions. The transactional outbox pattern is the right tool here: in the same database transaction that writes the domain change, you write an outbox row, and a separate process polls the outbox and publishes to the broker. This gives you exactly-once-in-effect semantics without the complexity of distributed transactions.

Once the events are flowing, the second stage is to write read-side projections that consume those events and build alternative representations of the data — denormalised views, search indexes, analytics tables. The projections live in their own service from the start, because they have a different scaling profile and don't share a database with the monolith. This stage is low-risk and produces immediate value, because the projections often replace expensive joins or batch jobs that were already painful.

The third stage is to start moving write paths out of the monolith. Pick a bounded context where the data and the logic are reasonably self-contained — billing is a classic choice — and extract it as a service that owns its data, exposes a synchronous API for commands, and emits domain events for everyone else. The monolith stops writing to the extracted tables and starts calling the new service. The data ownership boundary is the hard part; the wire protocol is the easy part.

The fourth stage, and the one most teams underestimate, is decommissioning. The old code paths inside the monolith have to be deleted, the old database tables have to be retired, and the old runbooks have to be updated. If you skip this, you end up with two systems doing the same thing, drift between them, and twice the operational burden. Plan the decommissioning as a first-class deliverable, not as cleanup work.

# 3. Patterns that survive

A small number of patterns do most of the heavy lifting and pay back the complexity they introduce.

The transactional outbox already mentioned. It's the only correct way to publish events without losing them or double-publishing under failure.

The saga: long-running workflows expressed as a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action. Use orchestrated sagas (a central coordinator) when the workflow is well-defined and you want a single place to reason about it. Use choreographed sagas (services react to each other's events) when the workflow is short and you want loose coupling. Don't mix the two styles in the same workflow; the resulting hybrid is impossible to follow.

The idempotency key on every command and every event. Every consumer must be able to receive the same message twice and produce the same effect once. The cheapest implementation is an idempotency table keyed by message ID with a TTL; the more sophisticated one ties idempotency to the business operation so that retries with different message IDs but the same intent are also deduplicated.

The schema registry. Events are a contract between services that live in different repositories and ship on different schedules. Without a registry that enforces backward compatibility, you will break consumers every time a producer ships, and you will discover this in production. Avro and Protobuf both have mature schema-registry tooling; JSON Schema works too if you're disciplined.

The consumer group with explicit offset management. Auto-commit is a foot-gun: it commits offsets before your handler has finished, which means a crash mid-handler turns into silent message loss. Always commit offsets explicitly after the handler succeeds, and design handlers to be idempotent so that the crash-before-commit case results in reprocessing, not loss.

# 4. Patterns that explode at scale

Some patterns look great in a tutorial and become operational liabilities at production scale.

The global event bus where every event goes to every service. It seems clean — fanout for free — but it makes versioning impossible because every change touches every consumer, and it makes capacity planning impossible because adding a consumer can saturate the broker. Keep events scoped to bounded contexts, with explicit cross-context translation when needed.

The big-bang CQRS rewrite where every read goes through a projection and every write goes through a command handler. CQRS is a powerful pattern, but it doubles the surface area, and most reads in most systems don't need it. Apply CQRS surgically to the domains where the read and write models genuinely diverge, and leave the rest alone.

The synchronous-over-async pattern where service A publishes an event, service B handles it, and service A then polls for the result. This pretends to be event-driven but inherits all the latency costs of asynchrony without any of the decoupling benefits. If you need a synchronous answer, use a synchronous call. Save events for places where the caller genuinely doesn't need to wait.

The events-as-database pattern where the event log is treated as the system of record and every read replays history. Event sourcing is a legitimate and powerful pattern in specific domains — finance, audit, collaboration — but in most domains the operational cost of replaying history for every query, and of evolving event schemas across years of accumulated history, dwarfs the architectural benefits. Use it when you specifically need its properties, not as a default.

# 5. Team shape

The architecture is a mirror of the org chart, and migration is also an org-chart change. The shift from monolith to event-driven services typically requires moving from a small number of large teams owning the monolith to a larger number of small teams each owning one bounded context. This is the Conway's Law in action: if you don't restructure the teams, the services will quietly recoalesce into a distributed monolith — services that look independent but ship together, deploy together, and break together.

The practical implications are that each new service needs a clear owner, an on-call rotation, a runbook, and a budget for operational work. If you can't fund that for every service you're about to create, create fewer services. The right number is the number you can responsibly operate, not the number that fits cleanly on the architecture diagram.

# 6. Failure rehearsals

The last and most important step is to rehearse the failures before they happen in production. Concretely: practice broker outages by stopping the broker in staging and watching what happens to producer and consumer code paths. Practice consumer crashes mid-handler and verify that messages are reprocessed rather than lost. Practice schema-evolution scenarios by deploying an incompatible change and watching consumers behave. Practice DLQ recovery by deliberately routing messages to the DLQ and stepping through the runbook. Practice cross-service rollback by deploying a bad version of one service and observing the impact on every other service.

Each of these rehearsals reveals gaps. The gaps are cheap to fix in staging and expensive to fix at 2am with a real incident. The teams that do this work routinely operate event-driven systems with less stress than teams that operate monoliths. The teams that skip it learn the same lessons under load, with users watching, and usually conclude — wrongly — that event-driven architecture is just intrinsically harder.

The pattern, in the end, is: invest the unglamorous work up front, migrate one bounded context at a time, decommission ruthlessly, and treat operations as a first-class concern. Do that and the architecture pays off. Skip any of it and the architecture becomes a cautionary tale at the next conference.
